story:
Gerry, the only hobgoblin in Underhill’s palace and its Librarian for three centuries, left the dining room and set out for his quarters above the library. The mountain’s heart twisted around him in restless corridors lit by copper and bone lanterns. As so often happened, the tunnels had shifted at the mountain’s whim, and he was lost again. He adjusted his tiny wire frames in his big hands and ignored the old whispers—the talk that hobgoblins were dangerous among the tall High Fae, that his secondary form could not be trusted. He’d grown Underhill’s collection into an enviable treasure and kept his boggart under control. That should count for something.

Late hour deepened the shadows that puddled in crevices and pooled beyond torchlight. He told himself they couldn’t touch him. Rounding a curve, he stopped at the statue of Underhill’s first queen. Her granite face, perfect and remote, stood sentinel over the hidden stairs to the catacombs, where queens lay in immortal sleep in stone and bone caskets. Her five-pointed crown brushed the alcove’s top. At her feet, the trapdoor—always flush with the floor, always sealed by magic—gaped open. It was hinged upright, teetering perpendicular like a jaw ready to bite, exposing a square of ebony with the suggestion of steps falling away into dark.

It was always closed. It should always be closed.

He leaned to peer in. His glasses slid from his nose, slipped through his fingers, and shattered. The world blurred and tilted. One of his eyes was green and mostly ordinary; the other, golden and tuned to magic, still needed lenses to focus. It had taken months to find frames. He swore, bumped the open hatch with his shoulder, and the hinges complained in a dull, warped creak.

A breath grazed the fine hairs of his ears, intimate as a lover’s whisper. The corridor remained empty and indistinct, yet a ribbon of air coiled up from the opening, a memory of a noose tightening, tugging his collar, straining buttons. Then came a scent: cardamom and sandalwood, the expensive perfume of mourning. He reached to shut the door.

“Leave it,” a voice murmured from below, musical and hollow, as if an echo had learned to speak.

Fog gathered in the opening, thickening from a smudge to a roiling, dirty white. Tendrils slid out, tasting the air, creeping across the floor toward his boots. The scent of funerals deepened, layered and costly. He hesitated.

“Leave the entrance,” the voice insisted.

His green eye whirled uselessly. He focused on his gold eye, asking it to pierce through the fog and darkness for what lurked below. An image formed, blurry but recognizable as the form of a High Fae, with long flaxen hair flowing over her face and shoulders, like a veil. Chills raced down his back. “Who are you?” he asked.

The hair parted. A beautiful skeleton smiled, skin pulled too tight over high bones, magic drifting in her hollow eye sockets like dust in angled light.

Panic cracked him open. The boggart surged. Muscle tore and knit. His voice snagged between a cry and a sob. Fear’s engine flooded him like harsh whiskey, into every pore and hair, reshaping him into whatever form would most terrify the thing below. When the rush receded, Gerry was a captive rider inside a body that cackled. His mouth was crowded with needles; his tongue, long and thin, tasted the damp with hunger. Half-foot claws flexed.

He plunged into the catacombs. In the dark, whatever nightmare the boggart chose, Gerry couldn’t see. He only felt the old queen’s retreat, fog snapping back, power stuttering like dry beads in a shaken gourd.

Even immortals have fears.

Above, the first queen’s stone gaze did not blink. The open hatch yawned, and the tunnels of Underhill shifted again, as if the mountain itself were listening.
